title: Find Min and Max with 3n/2 Comparisons

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Find both the minimum and maximum of n numbers using as few comparisons as possible.
rules: Naive: scan once for min (n-1 cmp), scan again for max (n-1 cmp). Total ~ 2n.
Pairing trick: process two elements at a time. Compare them (1 cmp), put smaller in MIN-candidates, larger in MAX-candidates.
Then min-of-MIN takes n/2 - 1 comparisons; max-of-MAX takes n/2 - 1.
setup.question: Show why the pairing approach uses ~ 3n/2 comparisons.

learningObjectives: Recognize the pairing pattern -- generic for tournament problems.
Match the lower bound of 3n/2 - 2 (provable by adversary argument).

stages.label: Counting comparisons
stages.prompt: n even. Pair up: n/2 comparisons (one per pair). Then find min over the n/2 'smaller' elements and max over the n/2 'larger' elements. How many comparisons in those two reductions?

options.text: Each is a min/max over n/2 elements: n/2 - 1 comparisons each. Sum: n/2 + 2*(n/2 - 1) = 3n/2 - 2.

stages.explanation: Min over k elements needs k-1 pairwise comparisons (linear scan). With k = n/2: n/2 - 1 each. Plus the n/2 pair comparisons: total = 3n/2 - 2 < 2n.

stages.label: Commit -- odd n
stages.prompt: n odd: leave one element out, pair the other n-1. Total comparisons?

options.text: (n-1)/2 pair comparisons + ((n-1)/2 - 1) for min + ((n-1)/2 - 1) for max + 2 to compare the leftover element against current min and max. Total = 3(n-1)/2.

stages.explanation: Odd case is roughly the same: ~3n/2 comparisons. Either way, both forms beat the naive 2n bound. The 3n/2 - 2 bound is also TIGHT -- adversary argument shows you can't do better.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Prove the matching LOWER bound of 3n/2 - 2 via the adversary method.
Generalize to find the second-largest in n + log_2 n - 2 comparisons (tournament method).
Why does this beat naive n-1 + n-1 = 2n? (Pairing 'reuses' one comparison to learn information about both extremes.)
